days.title: My parents: майка and баща

grammar.title: Saying who someone is with съм
grammar.explanation:
Bulgarian **has** a present-tense verb "to be". Its forms are: **съм** (I am), **си** (you are), **е** (he/she/it is), **сме** (we are), **сте** (you are, plural/polite), **са** (they are).

To point at a person and say who they are, use **Това е** ("This is") + the noun. The noun stays in its plain dictionary form — Bulgarian has **no noun cases**, so the word never changes its ending for grammatical role.

- **Това е майка.** — This is mother.
- **Това е баща.** — This is father.

To say "my", the short possessive **ми** comes after the noun: **майка ми** (my mother), **баща ми** (my father). This is the easiest way to show "my" and you will use it often.

reading.title: Моето семейство (My family)
reading.text: Това е майка ми. Това е баща ми. Това е моето семейство.
reading.translation: This is my mother. This is my father. This is my family.

days.speakingPrompt: Point at a photo (real or imagined) and introduce two people using "Това е ...". For example: "Това е майка ми. Това е баща ми."

days.title: Brother and sister: брат and сестра

grammar.title: съм + noun for relationships
grammar.explanation:
Use the verb **съм** with a noun to say what relationship someone is. The subject pronoun and the verb form must match:

- **Аз съм брат.** — I am a brother.
- **Ти си сестра.** — You are a sister.
- **Той е брат ми.** — He is my brother.
- **Тя е сестра ми.** — She is my sister.

The noun does **not** change form — Bulgarian has no cases. Notice the pronouns: **аз** (I), **ти** (you), **той** (he), **тя** (she).

Word order tip: **съм / си / е** usually cannot start a sentence. They lean on the word before them: "Аз **съм** брат", "Той **е** брат ми".

reading.title: Брат и сестра (Brother and sister)
reading.text: Това е брат ми. Той е брат ми. Тя е сестра ми.
reading.translation: This is my brother. He is my brother. She is my sister.

days.speakingPrompt: Say who you are in your family using "Аз съм ...", then introduce a sibling with "Той е брат ми" or "Тя е сестра ми".

days.title: Children: син and дъщеря

grammar.title: The definite article: a suffix on the noun
grammar.explanation:
Bulgarian has no separate word for "the". Instead, **"the" is a suffix** added to the end of the noun:

- **masculine**: -ът / -а — **син → синът** (the son)
- **feminine**: -та — **майка → майката**, **сестра → сестрата**, **дъщеря → дъщерята**
- **neuter**: -то — **дете → детето**

Compare:
- **Това е син.** — This is a son. (no article)
- **Синът ми е малък.** — My son is little. (definite + ми)

With **съм** you continue to match the verb to the subject: **Ти си** син ми (you are my son), **Ти си** дъщеря ми (you are my daughter).

reading.title: Синът и дъщерята (The son and the daughter)
reading.text: Това е син ми. Тя е дъщеря ми. Детето е малко.
reading.translation: This is my son. She is my daughter. The child is little.

days.speakingPrompt: Introduce two younger family members using "Той е син ми" and "Тя е дъщеря ми". Add the article once, e.g. "Синът ми е малък".

days.title: Husband and wife: съпруг and съпруга

grammar.title: съм in the plural: ние сме
grammar.explanation:
Now the plural forms of **съм**:
- **ние сме** — we are
- **вие сте** — you are (plural or polite)
- **те са** — they are

Use them with relationship nouns just like the singular:
- **Ние сме семейство.** — We are a family.
- **Той е съпругът ми.** — He is my husband.
- **Тя е съпругата ми.** — She is my wife.

The definite article suffix appears again: **съпруг → съпругът** (the husband), **съпруга → съпругата** (the wife). The noun ending shows "the"; there are still no cases.

reading.title: Ние сме семейство (We are a family)
reading.text: Той е съпругът ми. Аз съм съпругата му. Ние сме семейство и сме заедно.
reading.translation: He is my husband. I am his wife. We are a family and we are together.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe a married couple (real or imagined). Say "Той е съпругът ми / Тя е съпругата ми" and finish with "Ние сме семейство".

days.title: There is, there isn't: има and няма

grammar.title: има (there is) and няма (there isn't)
grammar.explanation:
To say something **exists**, use **има** ("there is / there are"). To say it **does not exist**, use **няма** ("there is no / there isn't"). Both are used the same way for singular and plural, and the noun after them does not change form:

- **Има едно дете.** — There is one child.
- **Има две деца.** — There are two children.
- **Няма брат.** — There is no brother.

You can add a place with a preposition — Bulgarian shows location with prepositions, not case endings:
- **У дома има две деца.** — At home there are two children.
- **В семейството няма син.** — In the family there is no son.

Irregular plural to know: **дете → деца** (child → children).

examples.english: At home there are two children.
In the family there is no brother.

reading.title: У дома (At home)
reading.text: У дома има майка, баща и две деца. Няма брат, има две сестри.
reading.translation: At home there are a mother, a father and two children. There is no brother, there are two sisters.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe who is in your family using "има" and "няма". For example: "Има майка и баща. Има две деца. Няма брат."

days.title: Do you have one? Asking with има/няма

vocab.exampleEn: I have a brother and a sister.
I do not have a brother.

grammar.title: Questions with има ли, and "I have" (имам)
grammar.explanation:
To ask a yes/no question, add the particle **ли** right after the verb:
- **Има ли брат?** — Is there a brother? / Is there a brother (in the family)?
- **Има ли деца?** — Are there children?

To ask "how many", use **колко**:
- **Колко деца има?** — How many children are there?

The same root gives you the personal verb **"to have"**: **имам** (I have), **нямам** (I do not have). The thing you have does not change form:
- **Имам брат и сестра.** — I have a brother and a sister.
- **Нямам син.** — I do not have a son.

Note the number agrees in gender: **един** брат, **една** сестра, **едно** дете.

examples.russian: Имам брат и сестра, нямам син.
examples.english: I have a brother and a sister, I do not have a son.

reading.title: Колко деца? (How many children?)
reading.text: — Има ли деца у дома? — Да, има две деца. — Имам син и дъщеря. Нямам брат.
reading.translation: "Are there children at home?" "Yes, there are two children." "I have a son and a daughter. I do not have a brother."

days.speakingPrompt: Ask and answer about a family. Use "Има ли ...?", "Колко деца има?", and answer with "Имам ..." and "Нямам ...".

days.title: Review: Family, съм, and има/няма

grammar.title: Week 9 recap: relationships, existence, and "have"
grammar.explanation:
This week you learned to talk about **family and people**.

**съм + noun for relationships** — the verb "to be" matches the subject; the noun never changes (no cases):
- аз **съм**, ти **си**, той/тя **е**, ние **сме**, вие **сте**, те **са**
- Аз съм брат. Тя е съпругата ми. Ние сме семейство.

**The definite article is a suffix:** masculine -ът/-а (синът), feminine -та (майката, сестрата, съпругата), neuter -то (детето), plural -те.

**има / няма** for existence — same form for singular and plural; add a place with a preposition (у дома, в семейството):
- Има две деца. Няма брат.

**Questions and "have":** add **ли** for yes/no (Има ли брат?), **колко** for "how many". Personal forms: **имам** (I have), **нямам** (I do not have).

**Family words:** майка, баща, брат, сестра, син, дъщеря, съпруг, съпруга, дете/деца, семейство.

examples.russian: Ние сме семейство: майка, баща и две деца.
examples.english: We are a family: a mother, a father and two children.
I have a sister, but I do not have a brother.

examples.russian: Има ли деца у дома? — Да, има две.
examples.english: Are there children at home? — Yes, there are two.

reading.title: Моето семейство (My family)
reading.text: — Колко деца има у дома? — Има две: син и дъщеря. — Имаш ли брат? — Не, нямам брат, имам сестра. Ние сме семейство и сме заедно.
reading.translation: "How many children are there at home?" "There are two: a son and a daughter." "Do you have a brother?" "No, I do not have a brother, I have a sister. We are a family and we are together."

days.speakingPrompt: Describe your family in 4-5 sentences. Use "съм" to say who you are (Аз съм ...), introduce two relatives (Той е ... / Тя е ...), and use "има" and "няма" to say who is and isn't in the family. End with "Ние сме семейство".
